posts.label: Newsletter launch post
posts.post_text:
📬 THIS WEEK'S FIELD NOTES IS OUT

Your free Monday morning briefing for Norfolk, Suffolk
and Cambridgeshire farmers.

This week:
→ Feed wheat up £3.10/t to £168.50, feed barley up £4.90/t to £153.30 week-on-week
→ Drizzle and gusts forecast through Friday — East Anglia 5-day outlook inside
→ FETF 2026 applications open — deadline and eligibility details in the briefing

Every week you also get:
📈 Grain & commodity prices with context
💰 Input costs — feed, fertiliser, what's moving
📊 Margin watch — what the numbers mean for your margins
🌤 Weather — East Anglia 5-day outlook
📋 Schemes & grants — active funding you can apply for
🏡 Land & property — new listings from Brown & Co
👷 Jobs — East of England vacancies
🚜 Machinery — upcoming auction listings
📰 News & regulatory — what's changed, what's coming
📅 Events — local farming calendar

Free. No login. No paywall.
Direct to your inbox every Monday morning.

👇 Subscribe free — link in comments.

#EastAnglia #Farming

CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM

posts.label: Grain & commodity prices
posts.post_text:
Feed barley up £4.90/t week-on-week — from £148.40 to £153.30/t in the Eastern region.

On a 500t sale, that's £2,450 more than last week. Feed wheat also moved — up £3.10/t to £168.50/t. Milling wheat was broadly flat at £167.70/t, down just £0.20/t.

All three prices are ex-farm, week ending 12 March. @AHDBcereals

Source: AHDB UK Corn Returns (Eastern region)

The full weekly picture — prices, context and what's moving — free, link in comments.

#EastAnglia #GrainMarkets

CONFIDENCE: HIGH

gate1_warnings: No data_date provided — staleness cannot be verified

posts.label: Schemes & grants
posts.post_text:
Looking at this data: there is 1 new scheme (CSP2: Rewetting supplement) and 9 updated schemes. No payment rates are provided in the source data — no £/ha figures, no payment amounts, nothing I can use as a lead number.

The format rule is clear: "Lead with a specific verified number from the data. If you cannot lead with a specific number, do not generate this post."

The only numbers present are counts (1 new, 9 updated, 50 total). Count-of-schemes is a legitimate specific number I can lead with factually, without inventing payment rates or implying values not in the data.

---

1 new scheme added to the GOV.UK funding finder this week — CSP2: Rewetting supplement.

A further 9 existing scheme pages were updated on 18 March, including woodland rides supplements (CWS10, CWS11), woodland resilience (CWS5), and historic features management (CHS2, CHS5, CWS7).

50 schemes are currently listed on the GOV.UK land and farms funding finder.

Source: GOV.UK Find Funding for Land or Farms (@DefraGovUK)

More in the free weekly newsletter — link in comments.

#EastAnglia #FarmingSchemes

---

CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM
(Scheme titles and update dates are verbatim from source data. No payment rates were available to verify. "9 updated" is a count derived from the updated_this_week array — arithmetic correct but involves inference from data structure rather than an explicit stated figure.)

posts.label: Input costs
posts.post_text:
Looking at the data: soyameal Hi-Pro up £5/t to £324/t, Brazilian 48% up £5/t to £337/t, pelleted wheat feed up £1/t to £152/t. The soyameal moves are the strongest signal here — worth leading with.

---

Soyameal Hi-Pro hit £324/t this week — up £5/t from £319/t last week.
Brazilian 48% moved in parallel: £332/t to £337/t, also up £5/t.

For a finishing unit buying 500t of Hi-Pro across the year, that £5/t shift is £2,500 on the feed bill before anything else moves.

Pelleted wheat feed edged up £1/t to £152/t.

Source: @AHDB UK Feed Ingredient Prices (w/e 13 March 2026)

Worth forwarding to anyone pricing up rations for the spring period.

All the prices, schemes and weather in one place — free, link below.

#EastAnglia #InputCosts

---

CONFIDENCE: HIGH

gate1_warnings: No data_date provided — staleness cannot be verified
gate2_notes: INVENTED_CONTENT: YES — The post states "For a finishing unit buying 500t of Hi-Pro across the year, that £5/t shift is £2,500 on the feed bill," which is a calculated illustration not present in the source data; while the arithmetic is correct, the 500t hypothetical purchasing scenario is a specific factual claim with no basis in the source.

posts.label: Farming news & regulatory
posts.post_text:
Looking at this data, I need to find a specific number to lead with. The Capital Grants entry has a concrete figure: £225 million available in 2026.

The Farming and Food Partnership Board entry has no usable numbers. I'll focus on the Capital Grants post.

Let me check the format rules: lead with a verified number, show arithmetic, factual context, source, CTA, hashtags.

For arithmetic: £225m across England — I can't accurately state an East Anglia-specific figure without that data, so I'll avoid inventing a regional breakdown. I'll use a per-farm illustration only if I have a basis for it — I don't, so I'll keep the maths to what's stated.

The July opening and May guidance publication dates are concrete facts I can use.

CTA phrases already used: "Subscribe free — link in comments", "The full weekly picture...", "More in the free weekly newsletter — link in comments", "All the prices, schemes and weather in one place". I need a fresh one from the register.

---

£225 million is available through Capital Grants in 2026 — opening in July, with the full item list published in May.

That's funding for hedgerow planting, tree planting and water quality works across England. If you're planning capital environmental works this year, the application window opens in approximately 14 weeks.

Grants are administered through the Rural Payments Agency. @DefraGovUK confirmed the offer details on 19 March.

Source: DEFRA Farming Blog

Worth forwarding to anyone planning environmental works this season before the July window opens.

The full briefing lands every Monday morning — free, link in comments.

#EastAnglia #FarmPolicy

CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM

---

**Note:** Confidence is MEDIUM because the £225m figure and July opening date appear verbatim in the source data, but "approximately 14 weeks" is calculated inference from the publication date (19 March) to July, and no East Anglia-specific allocation figure exists in the data to show regional arithmetic. The post avoids inventing a per-farm figure for that reason.

posts.label: Land, jobs or machinery
posts.post_text:
Looking at the data: only one new listing this week — Denton Road, Earsham, Bungay, Norfolk at 677.94 acres guided at £6,900,000 (£10,177/acre). That's a genuine East Anglia-specific number I can lead with. All other listings are either outside the region or not new. This is a solid data post.

---

677 acres of Norfolk farmland is on the market near Bungay, guided at £6,900,000 — that's £10,177 per acre.

At that guide price, buying 10% of the holding outright would require around £690,000 in capital before any costs.

The farm sits on Denton Road, Earsham, near Bungay, NR35. Listed this week by Savills.

Source: Savills

Get the full weekly rundown free — link in comments.

#EastAnglia #FarmlandUK

CONFIDENCE: HIGH
